journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of magnetic resonance imaging : JMRI

title-group.article-title: Enhancing Study Design and Analysis of MR Imaging Markers Through Measurement Error Modeling

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Quantitative Health Sciences, Cleveland Clinic, Cleveland, Ohio, USA
2Department of Biomedical Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio, USA
3Medical Scientist Training Program, Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine, Cleveland, Ohio, USA
4Heart, Vascular and Thoracic Institute, Cleveland Clinic, Cleveland, Ohio, USA
5Epilepsy Center, Neurological Institute, Cleveland Clinic, Cleveland, Ohio, USA
6Department of Radiology, Case Western Reserve University and University Hospitals Cleveland Medical Center, Cleveland, Ohio, USA
7Department of Neurosurgery, Duke University, Durham, North Carolina, USA
8Department of Biomedical Engineering, Duke University, Durham, North Carolina, USA

fn.p: Xiaofeng Wang and Walter Zhao contributed equally to this study.

X.W. and D.M. designed and directed the project. W.Z., T.S., Z.I.W., and D.M. provided key data and materials. W.Z., T.S., and Y.W. performed data curation. X.W. and Y.W. performed the statistical experiments and simulations. W.Z., X.W., Y.W., and D.M. analyzed the data. D.H.K. and M.A.G. provided input on experimental design. X.W., W.Z., Y.W., and D.M. interpreted the results of the study and wrote the manuscript. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the submitted manuscript.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Measurement error in imaging reduces statistical power and potentially biases parameter estimation, compromising study reliability.

sec.title: Purpose:
sec.p: To introduce a dual data collection design (reliability and main datasets) to quantify measurement error and apply regression calibration to correct error-prone imaging markers, thereby improving biomarker-outcome estimation, statistical power, and sample size planning.

sec.title: Study Type:
sec.p: Prospective (reliability) and retrospective (regression calibration).

sec.title: Population:
sec.p: 65 healthy volunteers (mean age: 23.2), 60 age and sex matched with 34 epilepsy patients (mean age: 28.7).

sec.title: Field Strength/Sequence:
sec.p: 3.0 T, MR fingerprinting (MRF) and T1-weighted (T1w) MPRAGE.

sec.title: Assessment:
sec.p: Three-dimensional brain scan-rescan data were acquired in 5 volunteers (6 identical acquisitions per volunteer across 3 scanners) to estimate reliability coefficients (λ) for MRF T1 and T1w signal intensity (SI) mean and standard deviation (SD). These coefficients were applied in regression calibration to correct imaging markers in the epilepsy cohort. Effect sizes for distinguishing lesional from control were compared before and after correction. Simulations evaluated the impact of additive and proportional bias on sample size, statistical power, and association estimates under single and multi-scanner scenarios.

sec.title: Statistical Test:
sec.p: Reliability coefficient, Cohen’s d, regression calibration, generalized estimation equations.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: MRF T1 markers exhibited higher reliability (λ = 0.887–0.941) than T1w SI markers with site effects (λ = 0.246–0.554). Regression calibration increased effect size more for T1w SI mean (333.22% increase) than for MRF T1 mean (12.57% increase). In multi-site simulations, regression calibration alone achieved unbiased estimate under small site effects (additive and proportional SD ≤ 0.2), whereas under larger site effects (additive SD ≥ 0.5) only the combined regression calibration and Combat produced near-zero bias (−0.024), outperforming naïve analysis (−0.423).

sec.title: Data Conclusion:
sec.p: The dual data acquisition design with regression calibration restores attenuated imaging biomarker associations, improves statistical power, and informs sampling requirements, thus enhancing reliability and generalizability in multi-site imaging studies.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Imaging has a fundamental role in medical decision-making, supporting clinical workflow from disease diagnosis to treatment planning, and outcome monitoring [1, 2]. Imaging studies provide non-invasive, data-rich measurements that, when analyzed together with disease characteristics and outcomes, may uncover specific patterns to guide clinical care. As a result, imaging markers have become, alongside molecular assays, part of the basis of robust response and progression criteria in clinical trials [3–6]. Recent technical advancements, such as the development of more informative imaging modalities, artificial intelligence (AI)-powered image analysis, and improved accessibility of imaging around the world, offer even broader cross-field impact. However, robust and generalizable statistical methods are needed to ensure reliable study design, biomarker development, and clinical decision support.

Measurement error is an important yet often underrecognized issue in imaging studies. Measurement error is defined as the difference between an observed biomarker value and its true value, and includes both systematic bias and random error [7]. In medical imaging, measurement error arises from multiple sources across the study pipeline, including differences in acquisition protocols, scanner hardware and software, processing algorithms, and manual operation. Figure 1A highlights measurement error sources along the clinical imaging workflow, which accrue at each step to contaminate downstream analysis. Critically, measurement error cannot be fully mitigated by larger sample sizes when imaging biomarkers serve as predictors or covariates. Figure 1B,C show a simple simulation demonstrating how measurement error introduces bias when estimating associations between imaging markers and study outcomes of interest. In this example, a strong linear relationship exists between the true imaging marker and study outcome (Figure 1B). However, measurement error causes bias in imaging marker values, indicated by data shifting along the x-axis (Figure 1C). Regression using error-prone data yields a much weaker trend (“bias toward the null”) and hides the true imaging marker-outcome association. Unmitigated, measurement error leads to biased parameter estimation, loss of statistical power to identify the associations between true imaging markers and outcomes, and masking of key imaging features: these effects all compromise the validity of the clinical studies and undermine the clinical utility of imaging marker studies [4, 6]. The full impact of measurement error from both systematic and non-systematic sources on different statistical situations is detailed in Table 1. While harmonization methods such as z-score normalization and ComBat [8–11] can reduce variability across sites in multi-site studies and standardizing datasets, they cannot correct site-specific errors or recover the true marker-outcome relationship. While statistical techniques for measurement variation and error correction have been developed in fields including epidemiology, nutrition, and environmental exposure [12–16], they have not been adopted in medical imaging, in part due to limited awareness of their necessity and effectiveness [4].

Thus the overall aim of this study was to introduce a framework that explicitly models and corrects for imaging measurement error in order to mitigate bias and improve the estimation of relationships between imaging markers and clinical outcomes. In particular, the aims were to build on the regression calibration approach [12, 15, 17] to quantify measurement errors in imaging methods using reliability indices and to correct for their effects, and also to provide guidance for incorporating measurement error modeling into clinical study design, including sample size planning and prediction model development.

sec.title: Measurement Error Correction Framework
sec.p: A dual data collection design was implemented to rectify imaging measurement error, consisting of (1) a main study dataset and (2) a reliability dataset (Figure 2). The main study dataset consisted of imaging data from study participants of interest where the primary outcome and error-prone imaging markers of interest were measured alongside covariates such as age and sex. The reliability dataset was obtained from a scan-rescan dataset where healthy subjects underwent repeated imaging across different scanners under the same conditions, enabling quantification of both systematic bias and random error. Main study imaging marker data was then calibrated using measurement error model estimates to achieve unbiased downstream analysis.

The following model of measurement error was used in this study. Figure S1 provides the directed acyclic graph illustrating the structure of the measurement error model. X denotes the true, unobserved value of an imaging marker which follows a continuous distribution with mean μ and variance σX2. The observed measurement X* is subject to additive measurement error U with the relationship between true and observed imaging marker values expressed as:
(1) X*=α+γX+U
where U follows a Gaussian distribution: U∼N0,σU2; α is the additive bias; and γ is the proportional bias. In this study, a simplified assumption of no proportional bias was made (γ = 1; proportional bias can be evaluated using a phantom study if available). Finally, Y denotes the outcome variable of interest and Z denotes other covariates (measured without error). The scan-rescan reliability part of the study enables the estimation of α and σU2, providing the necessary values for calibrating the error-prone imaging marker.

sec.title: Regression Calibration
sec.p:
Regression calibration was used to address study measurement error by substituting error-prone variables with estimates of their expected true values, derived from reliability data, in the outcome regression model [12]. This was accomplished in two key steps. First, an estimate of the true data was obtained from a calibration equation that weighs the observed values and the variable mean by the reliability coefficient:
(2) Xˆ=λX*+(1+λ)μˆX*−aˆ
where Xˆ is the estimated true data; X* is the observed, error-prone data, μˆX* is the variable mean value, αˆ is the estimated systematic bias, and
(3) λ=σX*2−σU2σX*2
is the imaging marker’s reliability coefficient, which can be estimated using the scan-rescan data. σX*2 is the variance of the error-prone variable.

Next, the estimated true data Xˆ was substituted for X* in the outcome regression analysis. By incorporating the adjusted covariate values, regression calibration reduces bias in estimating the association between covariates and outcomes [15]. This calibration step adjusted main study data toward their latent true values, thereby improving the validity of downstream analyses and ensuring that observed associations more closely reflect the underlying biological relationships. Bootstrapping can be employed to further estimate the regression coefficient’s covariance matrix in order to account for variation introduced by measurement error correction. Additional technical information is provided in Appendix S1.

sec.title: Sample Size Determination
sec.p:
Determining the sample size needed to achieve a desired statistical power is a critical aspect of imaging study design and can be optimized via measurement error correction. Measurement error causes the magnitude of observed associations to be weaker than the true associations: this effect size “attenuation” increases the probability of false negative findings (Type II errors) [18]. To achieve equivalent power despite reduced effect size, the sample size must increase. The compensatory increase in sample size is given by the inflation factor: IF=1/λ, where λ is the reliability coefficient defined in Equation (3) [19, 20]; thus, the adjusted sample size N is inversely related to the imaging marker’s reliability (more reliable data requires smaller samples) by:
(4) N=N0×IF=N0/λ
where N0 is the sample size needed without measurement error.

To determine the number of participants required for a repeatability study, we pre-specify a planned value of IF, IF0, and a relative deviation δ. Then the sample size needed for the repeatability study is [21].

A range of sample sizes for different expected inflated factors and CI widths are calculated and summarized in Table S1. For example, we expect to have an IF of 1.25 (i.e., λ=0.8), with a desired relative deviation δ=0.2. That is, the 95% CI of IF would span 1 to 1.5 (equivalently, the 95% CI of λ would span 0.67 to 1). The required sample size is n = (1.96/0.2)2 ((1.252−1)/1.25)2 = 19.4. Thus, 20 participants are needed in the repeatability study.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: The proposed measurement error correction framework was applied in a retrospective case study of MR fingerprinting (MRF) and MRI-based epileptic focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) lesion characterization [22–26]. Figure 2 details the overall study design including the main dataset of interest (epileptic lesion characterization) as well as the inclusion of a reliability dataset for imaging marker reliability estimation (healthy volunteer scan-rescan). The patient selection flowchart for the epileptic lesion study is shown in Figure S2. This retrospective, single site study was approved by the Cleveland Clinic Institutional Review Board and has been previously reported in a study focusing on developing a multiparametric MRI machine learning framework for focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) lesion characterization [25]. Regression calibration was accomplished using a reliability dataset of healthy volunteer scan-rescan data, acquired with written informed consent by local ethical approval.

Conventional T1-weighted MRI (T1w) is the reference standard for surgical evaluation of epileptic patients [27]; for a direct comparison, T1-based imaging markers from MRF data (MRF T1 mean and standard deviation (SD)) and from T1w signal intensity (SI) mean and SD were analyzed. Specifically, MRF T1 and T1w SI imaging markers were compared in terms of their ability to differentiate 3D epileptic lesion regions of interest (ROIs) from spatially matched brain ROIs in healthy controls. Imaging marker performance was compared before and after regression calibration. Imaging marker reliability coefficients calculated using 3D MRF T1 and T1w SI scan-rescan data from healthy controls (reliability dataset) were used for downstream effect size estimation and power analysis in the epilepsy study (main study). Demographic characteristics of all participants are summarized in Table S1.

sec.title: Reliability Study
sec.p: To measure the within-subject variability of MRF and MRI imaging markers for regression calibration, whole-brain 3D MRF and MRI data from 5 healthy control (HC) subjects was acquired prospectively in a multiparametric MRI scan-rescan dataset (convenience sampling) [28]. Each of the 5 healthy control subjects underwent 6 independent scan-rescan acquisitions with identical sequence protocols across 3 distinct 3 T scanners: 2 MAGNETOM Vida and 1 MAGNETOM Skyra Fit (Siemens Healthineers, Erlangen, Germany). MRF T1 maps and T1w images were further analyzed for regression calibration analysis and were chosen due to data availability in the main study dataset (other MRI sequences not acquired for all patients). The median time difference between scan-rescan acquisitions was 4 days. All imaging was performed using a 20-channel head coil and took place between December 2021 and June 2022. All subjects had no history of neurological or psychological disorders. This dataset was previously reported in a study analyzing the utility of a novel image discretization technique for improved radiomic feature reproducibility [28].

sec.title: Site Effect Analysis
sec.p: The impact of regression calibration on multi-site measurement error correction was evaluated using the multi-scanner reliability dataset. Imaging marker reliability coefficients were calculated under two scenarios: (1) treating data from all three scanners as originating from a single site (scanner effects not modeled) and (2) treating data from each scanner as originating from distinct sites (scanner effects explicitly modeled). Scanner effects were selected as a representative component of broader site effects because they are well defined and straightforward to implement experimentally. Throughout the remainder of the paper, scanner-dependent variation in imaging marker reliability is referred to as “site effects.”

sec.title: Epilepsy Case Study
sec.p: Patients with medically intractable epilepsy, who underwent pre-surgical evaluation at Cleveland Clinic between 2017 and 2020, were retrospectively selected using convenience sampling. Inclusion criteria included: (1) fulfillment of the International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) criteria [29] for medically intractable epilepsy; (2) confirmed unilateral focal epilepsy per consensus at the patient management conference (PMC); and (3) participation in an MRF scan as part of a research study. Exclusion criteria comprised: (1) visible abnormalities in the subcortical nuclei on conventional MRI with a standard epilepsy protocol; and (2) severe image artifacts preventing image segmentation or registration. Age- and sex-matched healthy volunteers were recruited as healthy controls (HCs). All subjects provided written informed consent prior to the MRF scan, and none of the study results influenced clinical decisions.

sec.title: Reliability Study
sec.p: MRF T1 and T1w MPRAGE images were obtained from each healthy subject (3 scanners, 2 scan-rescan acquisitions, 6 total acquisitions). Three distinct 3 T scanners: 2 MAGNETOM Vida and 1 MAGNETOM Skyra Fit (Siemens Healthineers, Erlangen, Germany) were used. MRF images were acquired with a spatial resolution of 1.0 × 1.0 × 1.0 mm3 with an acquisition time of 5 min 31 s. B1+ inhomogeneity was corrected using Bloch-Siegert B1+ mapping. MRF images were then reconstructed using the non-uniform fast Fourier transform (NUFFT) to generate T1 and T2 maps following neighborhood quadratic interpolation and dictionary matching via inner product search [22]. T1w magnetization prepared rapid acquisition gradient echo (MPRAGE) images were acquired with a spatial resolution of 1.0 × 1.0 × 1.0 mm3 with an acquisition time of 5 min 44 s. Additional acquisition and reconstruction details are provided in Zhao et al. [28].

MRF and T1w MPRAGE image data were processed following a standardized, per-subject workflow. For each subject, images across all acquisitions were first rigidly coregistered to each other. Images underwent brain extraction using an in-house tissue class-based segmentation method. Lastly, N4 bias field correction and z-score intensity standardization were applied to T1w images. The LONI Probabilistic Brain Atlas (LPBA) [30] atlas was then coregistered to generate corresponding brain ROIs of gray matter. A total of 56 gray matter regions were included for scan-rescan analysis. To reduce partial volume effects, brain tissue ROIs were eroded and smoothed by morphological closing to remove all voxels from a brain tissue ROI that were adjacent to another region. All ROIs were confirmed to have greater than 300 voxel count. MRF T1 and T1w SI mean and SD imaging values were then extracted from each ROI.

sec.title: Epilepsy Case Study
sec.p: MRF T1 and T1w MPRAGE images were obtained from epilepsy patients and HCs using a 3 T Siemens Prisma scanner (Siemens Healthineers, Erlangen, Germany). MRF images were acquired with a spatial resolution of 1.0 × 1.0 × 1.0 mm3 with an acquisition time of 10 min 24 s. A 3D B1+ mapping sequence with the same field of view and resolution as the MRF scan was acquired to correct B1+ inhomogeneity. MRF dictionary-based reconstruction was performed to generate T1 and T2 relaxometry maps for each subject. T1w MPRAGE images were acquired with a spatial resolution of 0.50 × 0.50 × 0.94 mm3 with an acquisition time of 3 min 59 s. Additional acquisition and reconstruction details are provided in Su et al. [25].

As described previously [26], a 3D ROI was manually created for each FCD lesion by a neuroimaging specialist (Z.I.W.) and confirmed by a board-certified neuroradiologist with 17 years of experience in epilepsy imaging. Lesion ROI delineation was based on the official consensus of positive/negative MRI findings at the epilepsy patient management conference and included areas of signal/morphometric changes on the pre-operative MRI, considering all available sequences (including T1w, T2w, and fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR)) from the clinical MRI protocol. However, T1w was the only sequence where data were successfully acquired for all patients, leading to its inclusion in study analysis.

Following ROI annotation, MRF T1 and T1w images were registered to Montréal Neurological Institute (MNI) standard space alongside corresponding ROI masks. Following z-score intensity standardization and outlier removal, MRF T1 and MRI T1w SI mean and SD values were extracted from ROIs. For each ROI, mean and SD values were extracted from the epileptic patient’s lesion (disease case) as well as from the corresponding anatomical region from the HCs. A total of 34 ROIs, each with 1 disease value and 60 HC values per imaging marker, were included in the analysis.

sec.title: Imaging Marker Reliability and Effect Size Analysis
sec.p: Using the scan-rescan dataset [28], reliability coefficients for MRF T1 and T1w markers were computed when considering scenarios with and without site effects (where site effects were scanner-dependent variations, see Appendix S1). Coefficients were then used in regression calibration to correct measurement error in the epilepsy study dataset. Next, generalized estimating equations (GEEs) were applied to compare the effect sizes (Cohen’s d) of uncorrected and corrected imaging markers in distinguishing lesional from normal brain regions.

For each imaging marker (MRF T1 and T1w SI mean/SD), reliability coefficient values were first calculated on a per-ROI-basis then pooled, with the mean reliability coefficient used for downstream analysis. Reliability coefficients were calculated under two scenarios: (1) site effects absent and (2) site effects present. For each marker, Cohen’s d values before and after calibration were reported for both scenarios (with and without site effects). Significance of effect size comparisons was determined by evaluating whether the 95% confidence interval of GEE model coefficients excluded zero. For all statistical testing, results were considered significant if p values were below the significance threshold p = 0.05.

sec.title: Power Analysis and Sample Size Simulation
sec.p: To assess the correction method’s utility for power and sample size estimation for future prospective studies involving imaging markers with varying reliability coefficients, each imaging marker’s power curve was simulated across multiple effect sizes (0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5). For each effect size, balanced imaging marker datasets (equal split of lesion and healthy control data) were synthetically generated by adding reliability-proportional measurement error to imaging markers. A GEE model was then fitted to detect mean differences between case and control groups using error-prone as well as error-free imaging data. This process was repeated 10,000 times for each effect size and sample size scenario, with the mean power plotted as a function of dataset sample size.

sec.title: Multi-Site Simulation
sec.p: The utility of the proposed framework for correcting multi-site measurement error was evaluated using a simulation study with five independent sites. Each site consisted of a primary dataset of 100 subjects and a corresponding reliability dataset of 10 subjects. Each subject was assigned a “true” continuous imaging marker, and disease status was generated from a logistic regression model with a true log-odds ratio of 1 per unit increase in the “true” marker, representing a moderate biomarker-outcome association. The observed imaging marker at each site was then perturbed by three distinct components of error: (1) a site-specific additive (fixed) bias, (2) a site-specific multiplicative (proportional) bias, and (3) random within-site measurement error with site-specific variance. In this formulation, each site had its own constant offset, its own scaling factor applied to the true biomarker values, and its own level of random noise, mimicking common sources of between-site variability such as differences in scanner calibration and measurement precision.

To parameterize these effects, the magnitude of the additive and multiplicative biases were varied across simulation scenarios. Site-specific additive biases were drawn from normal distributions centered at zero with standard deviations of 0 (none), 0.2 (low), 0.5 (moderate), or 0.8 (high). Site-specific multiplicative biases (scaling factors) were drawn from normal distributions centered at 1 with standard deviations of 0 (none), 0.1 (low), or 0.2 (moderate). This yielded 12 combinations of additive and multiplicative site-effect magnitudes, which are displayed in Figure 6. The scenarios with additive standard deviation ≤ 0.2 (top two rows of Figure 6) are referred to as “small” site effects, whereas scenarios with additive standard deviation ≥ 0.5 (bottom two rows) are referred to as “large” site effects. In addition, to represent general differences in measurement precision across sites, the standard deviation of the random measurement error was drawn independently for each site from a uniform distribution between 0.2 and 0.5.

Reliability datasets were generated under the same site-specific error structure. For each subject in the reliability sample at each site, two independent replicate measurements of the observed imaging marker were simulated using the same additive bias, multiplicative bias, and random error variance. These test–retest data were used to estimate site-specific measurement error variances and reliability coefficients, which in turn informed the regression calibration step.

For each of the 12 site-effect combinations, 1000 replicate multi-site datasets were generated. Within each replicate, the biomarker–outcome association under four analysis strategies were estimated: (1) a naïve model applied directly to the error-prone data without any adjustment; (2) ComBat harmonization across sites only; (3) regression calibration (measurement error correction) only; and (4) regression calibration followed by ComBat harmonization. Bias for each strategy was defined as the difference between the average estimated log-odds ratio across the 1000 replicates and the true log-odds ratio.

sec.title: Clinical Case Study: Epileptic Lesion Characterization
Imaging Marker Reliability and Effect Size
sec.p: Table 2 summarizes the scan-rescan reliability coefficients for MRF T1 and T1w SI imaging markers. Both MRF T1 mean and SD exhibited higher reliability coefficients than their T1w SI counterparts, with and without considering site effects due to variations across three scanners. While accounting for site effects had minimal impact on MRF T1 marker reliability (T1 mean/SD reduced from 0.890/0.948 to 0.887/0.941), T1w SI markers showed lower reliability with site effects present (SI mean/SD reduced from 0.781/0.873 to 0.246/0.554).

Figure 3 displays the effect sizes of MRF T1 and T1w SI markers before and after measurement error correction. Even before correction, MRF T1 mean, MRF T1 SD, and T1w SI mean were significantly associated with epileptic lesion status with MRF T1 mean showing the highest effect size (uncorrected Cohen’s d = 0.684). The positive effect size of MRF T1 mean suggests that higher T1 values increase the likelihood of an epileptogenic lesion; due to the inverse relationship between MRF T1 values and T1w SI, T1w SI mean’s effect sizes was negative, as expected. A positive association was observed between MRF T1 SD and lesion probability, while no significant association was found for T1w SI SD (p = 0.15). As shown in Table 3, error correction accounting for site effects markedly increased the T1w mean SI effect size (from −0.301 to −1.304), with only modest gains for MRF T1 marker effect sizes regardless of site effects.

sec.title: Power Analysis and Sample Size
sec.p: Figure 4 presents the baseline power curves (before measurement error correction) of each imaging marker across simulated effect size scenarios. For each scenario, the statistical power of uncorrected MRF T1 and T1w SI markers were compared to the power of an ideal marker (without error). As a result of their higher reliability coefficients, MRF T1 markers (mean λ = 0.887 and SD λ = 0.941) achieved much higher power than T1w SI counterparts (mean λ = 0.246 and SD λ = 0.554) at the same sample size. In contrast, less reliable T1w SI markers (mean λ = 0.246 and SD λ = 0.554) showed reduced statistical power at equivalent sample sizes. Overall, MRF T1 markers showed power close to that of an ideal marker (mean = 88.76% of ideal and SD = 94.86% of ideal) compared to T1w SI markers (mean = 36.60% of ideal and SD = 71.45% of ideal). In particular, T1w SI mean required approximately 4 times larger sample sizes than MRF T1 mean to achieve equivalent power.

The impact of measurement error correction on the power of MRF T1 and T1w SI imaging markers is shown in Figure 5. Given MRF T1 markers’ high reliability, regression calibration yielded a minor increase in overall power (mean = 10.68% increase, SD = 4.57% increase) while T1w SI marker power was greatly enhanced (mean = 219.08% increase, SD = 54.32% increase) to better align with ideal imaging marker performance.

sec.title: Multi-Site Measurement Error
sec.p: Figure 6 plots the regression parameter estimate biases under different combinations of additive and proportional bias site effects, comparing the performance of each estimator (with smaller bias indicating better accuracy). The detailed numerical results (means, 95% simulation intervals, medians, and interquartile ranges) are reported in Table S2. In scenarios with small site effects (SD of additive bias and proportional bias are less than 0.2), measurement error correction alone was sufficient to achieve unbiased estimation. In contrast, ComBat harmonization failed to remove bias under the aforementioned site effect scenarios. As the magnitude of additive bias site effects increased, neither ComBat nor measurement error correction alone were sufficient for accurate estimation (ComBat performed better when site effect became the major source of bias in the observed measurements). In these large site effect scenarios, combining both strategies (site-specific error correction, followed by ComBat harmonization) successfully recovered unbiased estimates.

sec.title: Computation Time
sec.p: All statistical analyses and simulation studies were performed on an institutional Linux server equipped with Intel(R) Xeon(R) Platinum 8260 CPUs running at 2.40 GHz (192 cores in total) and 3.0 TB of RAM, using R (version 4.3). The computation time for a single dataset analysis of the size considered in our epilepsy case study was less than 1 min, and the complete set of simulation experiments required less than 12 h of wall-clock time. The proposed measurement error regression calibration procedures are computationally lightweight, and for a moderate-sized study can typically be completed within a minute on a standard, moderately configured personal computer.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study introduced a regression calibration-based framework to correct measurement error issues commonly faced in medical imaging marker studies. Through case study and simulation, the developed method’s effectiveness for mitigating bias, improving the validity of statistical testing, and increasing statistical power was demonstrated, highlighting the positive impact of such correction strategies for strengthening downstream analysis.

sec.title: Imaging Marker Reliability
sec.p: Reliability reflects the consistency with which an imaging marker measures the same property across repeated assessments [31–33] and is crucial for evaluating robustness to extraneous factors such as scanner differences, protocol variations, or environmental conditions: it is also necessary to measure in order to apply the proposed error correction method. In this study, MRF T1 mean and SD markers showed superior reliability compared to their T1w SI counterparts in both the presumed absence and presence of site effects. This finding agrees with prior phantom [34] and in vivo [35–37] studies which have found MRF relaxation maps as well as downstream radiomic features [28, 38] to have improved repeatability and reproducibility over conventional weighted MRI sequences. This indicates that highly reliable imaging methods (such as MRF) may still achieve robust power even without error correction.

sec.title: Impact on Effect Size Estimation
sec.p: In the epilepsy cohort, the poor reliability of T1w SI markers resulted in severe attenuation of effect estimates. For T1w SI mean, the uncorrected effect size was underestimated to about a third of the true value which greatly downplayed its predictive utility compared to competing imaging markers. While regression calibration did successfully recover the true association between imaging markers and epileptic lesion status, T1w SI markers demonstrated wider confidence intervals as a result of greater estimation uncertainty in the presence of site effects. This directly stems from the poorer reliability of T1w SI markers during regression calibration [12, 17, 19]. Thus, while the corrected T1w SI mean effect size is statistically significant and large, its diagnostic utility is compromised by its greater uncertainty. This result underscores the importance of accounting for site effects during error correction to avoid underestimating the true strength of imaging associations.

sec.title: Impact on Statistical Power and Sample Size Estimation
sec.p: By attenuating study power, measurement error consequently inflates the required sample size to achieve a given power level. In the classical additive error framework [12–14], effective sample size is reduced proportionally to marker reliability: a 1/λ-fold increase in sampling is needed to achieve equivalent power. In this study, MRF T1 markers showed high reliability resulting in modest sampling inflation (approximately 10% for MRF T1 mean), while T1w SI mean’s low reliability would require a 4 times larger sample size to achieve similar performance. Correcting for measurement error in studies may help avoid the need for unnecessarily large study cohorts, reducing logistical costs and complexity [39].

sec.title: The Need for Measurement Error Correction in Multi-Site Studies
sec.p: The problem of measurement error is equally problematic in not only single site studies but also multi-site studies. As shown via simulation, regression calibration alone is sufficient to accurately estimate effect size when the level of additive and/or proportional bias is low. When site effects are large, regression calibration is complementary to data harmonization (both are needed) for correction, with data harmonization being insufficient when applied alone in all tested site effect scenarios. While data harmonization strategies such as ComBat [9, 40, 41] remain popular for multi-site analysis, these findings suggest that measurement error correction provides a unique benefit for multi-site study analysis that is unachievable otherwise. Notably, there may be considerable benefit to applying regression calibration in clinical trial studies which often adopt multi-site study designs [3, 4, 6]: the dual data collection design of a main study of interest and reliability study for calibration is easily achievable through addition of an additional scan-rescan study. It is critical to note that scan-rescan data can be acquired as part of, before, and after acquisition of the main study dataset, as long as scanner and other acquisition settings are unchanged. This flexibility allows for regression calibration to be applied to both prospective and retrospective data.

The proposed framework can also be adapted to single site multi-reader studies by treating each reader as analogous site and estimating the reader-specific additive and proportional bias components. In this setting, regression calibration would correct for the inter-reader variability following the same mechanism it corrected the inter-site variability in this study, enabling unbiased effect estimation even when reader effects are substantial. This extension of the developed method requires no conceptual modifications and can be directly applied to imaging studies involving any combination of scanners, human readers, and sites.

sec.title: Implementing Measurement Error Correction in Imaging Marker Studies
sec.p:
This study demonstrates the need to systematically address measurement error when designing and analyzing imaging marker studies. The following summarizes the key aspects necessary for successful implementation:
Strive for Robust Imaging Measurement: Use imaging modalities with inherently higher reliability, where researchers can minimize sample size inflation to reduce costs and improve statistical robustness.
Quantify Imaging Marker Measurement Error: Obtain a quantifiable, objective assessment (reliability coefficient) of imaging marker robustness in order to inform subsequent adjustments in study design and analysis.
Adjust Sample Size: Recognizing that measurement error attenuates effect estimates and reduces power, compensate using the sample size inflation factor (IF = 1/λ) to ensure studies are adequately powered.
Apply Statistical Corrections During Analysis: Implement measurement error correction methods like regression calibration to obtain unbiased estimates of imaging marker and clinical outcome associations and enhance interpretability.

The proposed measurement error correction method is a generalized framework for enhanced medical image analysis: while demonstrated here in an MRF and MRI-based epilepsy study, it readily extends to other imaging modalities and organ systems. Because correction is performed directly using imaging marker data, this method is applicable to measurement differences arising from all aspects of the imaging workflow, with the potential to improve disease diagnosis, treatment, and management.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: First, this study’s reliability dataset had a relatively small sample size of young healthy volunteers which negatively impacts the stability of reliability coefficient values; larger studies are needed to better estimate reliability coefficients across diverse populations and platforms. Second, analysis focused on a specific clinical application of epileptic lesion characterization using quantitative MRF and conventional weighted MRI. While the principles of measurement error correction are broadly applicable, the degree to which these findings can be generalized to other imaging modalities and clinical contexts requires further investigation. Third, the annotation of epileptic lesions was performed and confirmed by a neuroimaging specialist–neuroradiologist pair rather than by two independent observers. Bias in this labeling strategy may also contribute to measurement error which could not be accounted for by scan-rescan reliability analysis. Future studies may benefit from performing an additional inter-reader reliability study for additional correction. Fourth, regression calibration’s utility was demonstrated in the presence of additive, normally distributed measurement error, assuming no proportional bias. While not demonstrated here, proportional bias is correctable using paired measurements of error-prone markers and reference standards from reliability datasets, estimating intercept and slope for main study data adjustment. More sophisticated strategies may be necessary for complex error modeling scenarios.

Future research should explore the application of measurement error correction methods in longitudinal studies and multicenter trials, where variability in imaging protocols and equipment can further exacerbate measurement error. Additionally, developing standardized protocols for estimating reliability coefficients and implementing measurement error corrections can enhance the reproducibility and comparability of imaging marker studies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Regression calibration using scan-rescan reliability data has the potential to restore attenuated imaging biomarker associations, improve statistical power, and inform sampling requirements.

sec.title: Supplementary Material

caption.p: Appendix S1: Measurement error models and regression calibration.

Table S1: Required sample sizes for repeatability study across a range of pre-specified values of the inflation factor (IF0) and different 95% confidence intervals. Note that reliability coefficients λ0 =1/IF0.

Figure S1: Modeling measurement error in imaging marker studies. The association between an outcome variable and an imaging marker with measurement error is visualized using a directed acyclic graph with the following nodes: X, the true, unobserved value of the QIB; X*, the observed, error-prone measurement of the imaging marker; U, imaging measurement error; Y, the outcome variable; and Z, other covariates that may influence the outcome Y.
Table S2: The numerical summaries (means, 95% simulation intervals, median and interquartile ranges) for the multi-site simulation (corresponding to Figure 6).
Figure S2: Inclusion flowchart for the epilepsy case study. FCD = focal cortical dysplasia, HC = healthy controls, MRF = magnetic resonance fingerprinting.

sec.p: Additional supporting information can be found online in the Supporting Information section.

ack.title: Funding
ack.p: This work was supported by NIH grants F30 CA294611, R01 CA269604, R01 CA282516, R01 CA292091, R01 HD112923, R01 NS109439, T32 EB007509, T32 GM007250, TL1 TR002549.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Study protocols, developed models, and software are available upon reasonable request.

caption.p: (A) Sources of measurement error in clinical imaging studies. Measurement error can arise at each step of the imaging workflow and accrue to severely contaminate downstream modeling and analysis. For example, patient motion or scanner hardware issues during data acquisition can lead to error in imaging markers measurement, while differences in processing and analysis procedures can lead to further inaccuracy. Unaccounted for, measurement error leads to biased parameter estimation, loss of statistical power to identify the associations between true imaging markers and the outcome, and masking of key data features. These effects all limit the downstream applicability of imaging marker studies to inform clinical decisions. (B) Measurement error may introduce significant bias when estimating associations between the imaging marker and study outcomes. In this example, a strong linear relationship exists between the true imaging marker and study outcome. (C) However, measurement error causes large imaging marker bias, indicated by shifting along the x-axis. Regression using the error-prone imaging marker results in a much weaker linear trend (“bias toward the null”) and hides the true imaging marker-outcome association.

Study design overview. The effectiveness of the introduced regression calibration method is demonstrated in an epilepsy neuroimaging case study (main study). A one vs. many lesion analysis was performed where each epilepsy patient’s epileptic lesion (n = 34) was compared to spatially matched regions of interest (ROIs) from healthy volunteers (n = 60). Using scan-rescan data from healthy controls (n = 5), the bias and reliability of MRF and MRI T1-based imaging markers were estimated. Measured imaging marker bias was then applied to correct imaging marker data used for epileptic lesion characterization. The impact of measurement error correction on effect size, power calculation, and sample size estimation was evaluated.

MRF T1 and T1w SI marker effect sizes before and after regression calibration correction. Prior to correction, MRF T1 mean and SD were positively associated with lesion probability, while T1w SI mean was negatively associated; T1w SI SD showed no significance (confidence interval includes null). While regression calibration modestly increased MRF T1 effect sizes under both site effect scenarios, T1w SI mean’s effect size substantially improved when accounting for site effects (blue) demonstrating severe effect size underestimation in the presence of measurement error. (SD = standard deviation; SI = signal intensity).

Power analysis of measurement error correction across MRF T1 and T1w SI markers. Each curve represents each imaging marker’s power to detect a significant statistical difference between disease and control groups for varying effect sizes (Cohen’s d = 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5). Both MRF T1 mean and SD power curves (red) closely aligned with ideal performance (no error; blue) while T1w SI power curves (green) showed substantially reduced power at equivalent sample sizes (mean = 36.60% of ideal and SD = 71.45% of ideal). For T1w SI mean specifically, a much larger sample size (approximately 4 times larger) was needed to match MRF T1 mean’s power. (SD = standard deviation; SI = signal intensity).

Imaging marker power curves before and after correction (d = 0.5, site effects present). Regression calibration had minimal impact on the power of MRF T1 mean and SD as a result of high baseline reliability. In comparison, T1w SI markers showed notable improvement: for both mean and SD, the corrected power (green) overlaps with ideal performance (blue) indicating successful recovery of attenuated power. With low true reliability and limited sample size, reliability estimation is unstable; this instability can translate into over-correction in the regression-calibrated and inflated power. Collecting additional repeated measures improves the stability of reliability estimation and improves the small-sample inference. (SD = standard deviation; SI = signal intensity).

Bias plots for regression parameter estimates for each combination of additive and proportional bias site effects. In each scenario, the naive estimate, estimate following ComBat data harmonization, estimate following measurement error correction, and estimate following measurement error correction and ComBat were compared. For scenarios with small site effects (top two rows), measurement error correction outperformed ComBat and alone was sufficient to achieve unbiased estimation. For large site effect scenarios (bottom two rows), neither ComBat nor measurement error correction alone were sufficient: site-specific measurement error correction followed by ComBat successfully recovered the unbiased estimate in all scenarios. True β: Oracle estimate using the unobserved, error-free feature values. Naïve β^: estimate from observed feature values still containing measurement error and site effects. Harmonized β^: estimate after removing site-specific effects via harmonization; measurement error remains uncorrected. Corrected β^: regression-calibrated estimate correcting for measurement error; site effects are left unadjusted. Corrected then harmonized β^: first perform regression calibration for measurement error, then apply harmonization to the corrected data. (SD = standard deviation; SI = signal intensity).
Effects of measurement error on statistical analysis.

tr.td: Comparing continuous outcomes measured with error
Regression with error-prone outcome (no covariate error)
Regression with error-prone outcome and covariates

fn.p: Note: The power of a test is only meaningful when null hypothesis testing is valid.

caption.p: Imaging marker reliability coefficients in the presence of site effects.

fn.p: Note: Reliability coefficients were computed in the absence and presence of site effects. Site effects absent = three scanners treated as one site. Site effects present = three scanners treated as three independent sites.
Abbreviations: MRF = MR fingerprinting, SD = standard deviation, T1w = T1-weighted.

caption.p: Imaging marker effect sizes for epileptic lesion characterization.

fn.p: Note: Data in brackets are ranges. Significant effect sizes (ranges not crossing zero) are bolded.
Abbreviations: MRF = MR fingerprinting, SD = standard deviation, SI = signal intensity, T1w = T1-weighted.

boxed-text.p: Imaging plays an essential role in disease diagnosis and management but relies on subjective assessment by radiologist experts. A major problem limiting the use of objective, quantitative numbers in imaging is measurement error, which arises from factors like scanner performance or patient motion and weakens statistical findings. This study introduces a way to correct for measurement error in imaging studies using regression calibration. In an epilepsy case study, the proposed measurement error correction framework successfully improved the accuracy of study findings including imaging marker effect size, statistical power, and sampling estimation, with potential application for improving clinical trial study designs.
